content: Personal Pronouns mina (ma) — I sina (sa) — you tema (ta) — he/she/it meie (me) — we teie (te) — you (pl/formal) nemad (nad) — they Short forms (ma, sa, ta...) are used in everyday speech. 💬 Ma olen tudeng. — I am a student.

Verb: olema (to be) — Present ma olen — I am sa oled — you are ta on — he/she is me oleme — we are te olete — you are nad on — they are Negative: ei ole (for all persons) 💬 Ma olen eestlane. — I am Estonian. 💬 Ta ei ole kodus. — He/she is not at home.

Basic Word Order: SVO Estonian generally follows Subject-Verb-Object: 💬 Ma söön leiba. — I eat bread. 💬 Ta loeb raamatut. — He reads a book. In questions, the verb comes first: 💬 Kas sa räägid eesti keelt? — Do you speak Estonian? 'Kas' is the yes/no question word.

Nominative Case (nimetav) The base/dictionary form. Used for: • Subject of a sentence • After 'olema' (to be) 💬 Koer on suur. — The dog is big. 💬 See on raamat. — This is a book. No endings added — this is the form you see in the dictionary.

Genitive Case (omastav) Shows possession and is the stem for many other cases. koer → koera (dog's) raamat → raamatu (book's) maja → maja (house's) 💬 See on koera nimi. — This is the dog's name. 💬 Raamatu hind on kõrge. — The book's price is high. Genitive is the most important case to learn — it forms the base for other cases.

Partitive Case (osastav) Used for: • Partial/uncounted objects • After numbers (except 'üks') • Negative sentences koer → koera raamat → raamatut piim → piima 💬 Ma joon piima. — I drink milk. 💬 Mul ei ole raamatut. — I don't have a book. 💬 Kaks koera. — Two dogs.

Question Words kes? — who? mis? — what? kus? — where? (location) kuhu? — where to? (direction) kust? — where from? millal? — when? kuidas? — how? miks? — why? kui palju? — how much/many? 💬 Kus sa elad? — Where do you live? 💬 Mis see on? — What is this?
Numbers 1-10 1 üks 2 kaks 3 kolm 4 neli 5 viis 6 kuus 7 seitse 8 kaheksa 9 üheksa 10 kümme After numbers 2+, nouns take the partitive singular: 💬 kolm raamatut — three books

Negation with 'ei' Estonian uses ei + verb stem (no personal endings): ma söön → ma ei söö sa räägid → sa ei räägi ta loeb → ta ei loe 💬 Ma ei söö liha. — I don't eat meat. 💬 Ta ei räägi eesti keelt. — He doesn't speak Estonian. The verb loses its personal ending after 'ei'.

Simple Past (lihtminevik) Formed with -s/-si suffix: ma sõin — I ate sa sõid — you ate ta sõi — he/she ate me sõime — we ate te sõite — you ate nad sõid — they ate Negative: ei söönud (for all persons) 💬 Ma sõin eile suppi. — I ate soup yesterday. 💬 Ta ei söönud midagi. — He didn't eat anything.

Present Perfect (täisminevik) Formed with olema + -nud participle: ma olen söönud — I have eaten sa oled söönud — you have eaten ta on söönud — he/she has eaten Negative: ei ole söönud 💬 Ma olen Tallinnas elanud viis aastat. — I have lived in Tallinn for five years. 💬 Kas sa oled seda filmi näinud? — Have you seen this movie?

Illative Case — movement INTO Short illative (most common): maja → majja (into the house) linn → linna (into the city) kool → kooli (into the school) Long illative (-sse): raamatukogu → raamatukokku 💬 Ma lähen linna. — I'm going to the city. 💬 Lapsed lähevad kooli. — Children go to school. Illative answers: kuhu? (where to?)

Inessive (-s) — IN / Elative (-st) — OUT OF Inessive (kus? — where?): linn → linnas (in the city) kool → koolis (in school) maja → majas (in the house) Elative (kust? — where from?): linn → linnast (from the city) kool → koolist (from school) 💬 Ma elan Tallinnas. — I live in Tallinn. 💬 Ta tuleb koolist. — He comes from school.

Surface Cases (outer local cases) Allative (-le) — ONTO (kuhu?): laud → lauale (onto the table) Adessive (-l) — ON (kus?): laud → laual (on the table) Ablative (-lt) — OFF (kust?): laud → laualt (off the table) 💬 Pane raamat lauale. — Put the book on the table. 💬 Raamat on laual. — The book is on the table. 💬 Võta raamat laualt. — Take the book off the table.

Postpositions (come AFTER the noun) With genitive: maja ees — in front of the house maja taga — behind the house maja kõrval — next to the house maja sees — inside the house maja peal — on top of the house maja all — under the house 💬 Auto on maja ees. — The car is in front of the house. 💬 Kass istub laua all. — The cat sits under the table. Unlike English, Estonian puts these AFTER the noun.

ma-infinitive vs da-infinitive ma-infinitive — used for purpose/going to do: 💬 Ma lähen sööma. — I'm going to eat. 💬 Ta tuli õppima. — He came to study. da-infinitive — used after modal verbs: 💬 Ma tahan süüa. — I want to eat. 💬 Sa pead õppida. — You have to study. 💬 Kas sa oskad ujuda? — Can you swim? Rule of thumb: movement verbs → ma, modal verbs → da

Comparatives & Superlatives Comparative: genitive + -m Superlative: kõige + comparative suur → suurem → kõige suurem (big → bigger → biggest) ilus → ilusam → kõige ilusam (beautiful → more beautiful → most beautiful) 💬 Tallinn on suurem kui Tartu. — Tallinn is bigger than Tartu. 💬 See on kõige ilusam linn. — This is the most beautiful city.

Numbers 11-100 11 üksteist, 12 kaksteist... 20 kakskümmend 21 kakskümmend üks 30 kolmkümmend 100 sada Ordinals (formed with -s): 1st esimene 2nd teine 3rd kolmas 4th neljas 5th viies 💬 Ma elan kolmandal korrusel. — I live on the third floor.

Conditional Mood — would Formed with -ks suffix: ma sööksin — I would eat sa sööksid — you would eat ta sööks — he/she would eat me sööksime — we would eat Negative: ei sööks 💬 Ma tahaksin kohvi. — I would like coffee. 💬 Kas sa tuleksid minuga? — Would you come with me? Used for polite requests and hypotheticals.

Passive Voice Present passive: -takse/-dakse 💬 Siin räägitakse eesti keelt. — Estonian is spoken here. 💬 Ukse avatakse kell 9. — The door is opened at 9. Past passive: -ti/-di 💬 Maja ehitati 1990. aastal. — The house was built in 1990. No subject needed — the action itself is emphasized.

Translative Case (-ks) — becoming/turning into õpetaja → õpetajaks (becoming a teacher) haige → haigeks (becoming sick) suureks → suureks (becoming big) 💬 Ta õpib arstiks. — He's studying to become a doctor. 💬 Ma jäin haigeks. — I got sick. 💬 Laps kasvab suureks. — The child is growing up. Answers: kelleks? milleks? (into what?)

Three Less Common Cases Terminative (-ni) — up to/until: 💬 Ma jooksen metsani. — I run up to the forest. 💬 Õhtuni on aega. — There's time until evening. Essive (-na) — in the role of: 💬 Ta töötab õpetajana. — He works as a teacher. Abessive (-ta) — without: 💬 Ma tulin rahata. — I came without money. 💬 Probleemita! — No problem!

Relative Clauses kes — who (for people): 💬 Mees, kes seal istub, on mu isa. — The man who sits there is my father. mis — which/that (for things): 💬 Raamat, mida ma loen, on huvitav. — The book that I'm reading is interesting. kus — where: 💬 Linn, kus ma elan, on ilus. — The city where I live is beautiful. Note: 'mis' declines — mida (partitive), mille (genitive), etc.

topic: Verb Rection — Which Case to Use
content: Verb Rection Some verbs require specific cases: Partitive verbs (always partitive object): armastama → Ma armastan sind. ootama → Ma ootan bussi. Genitive → completed action: 💬 Ma lugesin raamatu läbi. — I read the book (finished it). Partitive → ongoing/partial action: 💬 Ma lugesin raamatut. — I was reading the book. This genitive/partitive distinction is one of Estonian's trickiest features!

Reported Speech — quotative mood Formed with -vat suffix: 💬 Ta olevat haige. — He is said to be sick. 💬 Ilm minevat külmaks. — The weather is said to be getting cold. Past: olevat + -nud: 💬 Ta olevat tulnud Eestist. — He is said to have come from Estonia. Used when reporting what someone else said, without confirming it's true.

Comitative Case (-ga) — with/together with Genitive + -ga: auto → autoga (by car/with the car) sõber → sõbraga (with a friend) 💬 Ma tulen autoga. — I'll come by car. 💬 Ta elab koos sõbraga. — He lives with a friend. 💬 Mis sinuga juhtus? — What happened to you? -ga is added to the genitive form. This is the 14th and final Estonian case!

Participles as Adjectives -nud (active, past): 💬 Väsinud mees istus pingile. — The tired man sat on the bench. -tud (passive, past): 💬 Suletud uks. — A closed door. 💬 Hästi tehtud! — Well done! -v (active, present): 💬 Magav laps. — A sleeping child. -tav (passive, present): 💬 Loetav raamat. — A readable book.

Imperative — Commands Singular (sa): verb stem 💬 Tule siia! — Come here! 💬 Istu maha! — Sit down! Plural/formal (te): stem + -ge/-ke 💬 Tulge siia! — Come here! (pl) 💬 Istuge maha! — Sit down! (pl) Negative: ära/ärge + verb stem 💬 Ära mine! — Don't go! 💬 Ärge unustage! — Don't forget! (pl) Let's: stem + -gem/-kem 💬 Lähme! — Let's go!

Common Word Formation Patterns Compound words: raamat + kogu → raamatukogu (library) linna + hall → linnahall (city hall) -ja (doer): õpetama → õpetaja (teacher) -mine (action noun): õppima → õppimine (studying) -lik (adjective): sõber → sõbralik (friendly) -tu (without): süü → süütu (innocent) -kas (having quality): jõud → jõukas (wealthy) Estonian builds long compound words — break them into parts to understand them.

Connecting Clauses Coordinating: ja — and aga/kuid — but või — or ning — and (formal) Subordinating: et — that sest/kuna — because kui — when/if kuigi — although selleks et — in order to 💬 Ma õpin, sest tahan eksamit sooritada. — I study because I want to pass the exam. 💬 Kuigi sajab, me lähme välja. — Although it rains, we'll go out.

Pluperfect — had done Formed with olin/olid/oli + -nud: ma olin söönud — I had eaten sa olid söönud — you had eaten ta oli söönud — he/she had eaten 💬 Kui ma tulin, ta oli juba lahkunud. — When I arrived, he had already left. 💬 Me olime söönud, enne kui nad tulid. — We had eaten before they came. Used for actions completed before another past action.

Estonian Object Cases — Summary Nominative object (full, imperative/impersonal): 💬 Ava uks! — Open the door! Genitive object (completed single action): 💬 Ma avasin ukse. — I opened the door. Partitive object (ongoing/partial/negative): 💬 Ma avan ust. — I'm opening the door. 💬 Ma ei avanud ust. — I didn't open the door. The three-way object distinction (nominative/genitive/partitive) is the hallmark of Estonian grammar.
